main_summary.summary: Junius Williams, interviewed July 20, 2011 by Joe Mosnier on the Rutgers University Newark campus, traces his path from segregated Richmond, Virginia, where his father was the city's first Black band director and family friend Oliver Hill was a neighbor, through Amherst College, Yale Law School, and a half-century of organizing in Newark, New Jersey. Williams describes his February 1965 Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution conference at Mount Holyoke, headlined by Ossie Davis after Malcolm X and Michael Harrington could not appear; his March 1965 drive south with six Amherst-area students for the Selma to Montgomery March; his seven days in Kilby Prison alongside Worth Long and Stu House; and Tom Hayden's recruitment of him to the Newark Community Union Project in summer 1965. He details the post-Newark Rebellion fight against the College of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey land seizure that produced the 1968 medical school agreement on housing, jobs, and community control; his role as Kenneth A. Gibson's Model Cities director and subsequent firing; his 1978 election as the youngest National Bar Association president; the NBA's Zimbabwe independence paper adopted by the United Nations; and his current work training parents and youth organizers around Abbott v. Burke at Rutgers-Newark.
key_themes: Northern urban organizing and the class-versus-race analysis
Newark Community Union Project and SDS Northern strategy
Selma to Montgomery March and Kilby Prison
College of Medicine and Dentistry fight and post-rebellion community control
National Bar Association leadership and African independence advocacy
main_summary.historical_significance: Williams's testimony documents the migration of Movement experience from the Southern direct-action front to Northern community organizing — a transition often treated as discontinuous but which he experienced as continuous, with the Newark Community Union Project, the Newark Area Planning Association, and the Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal as direct successors to SNCC organizing methods. His account of the seven days in Kilby Prison with Worth Long and Stu House, the 1968 medical school agreement that produced 60 acres of community-controlled housing land and a construction-trades training program, and the National Bar Association's role in the Zimbabwe independence question provides primary-source documentation of three under-archived chapters in Civil Rights history: the Northern transition, the post-1967-rebellion negotiated settlements, and Black professional-association engagement with African decolonization.

chapters.title: Richmond Childhood and Family Music
chapters.summary: Williams describes his birth in Suffolk, Virginia in 1943, his parents' careers as music teachers (his father the first Black band director in Richmond, his mother a music coordinator), his Howard-and-Penn-educated mother's plans for his education, attending Armstrong High School over Maggie Walker, the Armstrong-Walker football classic at city stadium, and the Tobacco Festival Parade integration. Family friend Joe Kennedy taught music at Walker; Arthur Ashe was an early tennis acquaintance.

chapters.title: Early Civil Rights Awareness in Richmond
chapters.summary: Williams recalls the Emmett Till photograph in Jet magazine, Montgomery bus boycott news, the integration of Richmond buses, and Charles Sherrod's Virginia Union sit-ins at downtown department stores in 1960-61. His parents kept him out of the high school demonstrations using a fabricated claim that college students excluded high schoolers. Family friend Oliver Hill, whose firm Hill, Tucker and Marsh argued equal-pay cases for teachers, shaped Williams's path toward law.

chapters.title: Amherst College and Class Analysis
chapters.summary: Williams describes the culture shock of arriving at Amherst in 1961 with a four-student-per-class Black quota, joining Alpha Delta Phi as their first Black member over Theta Delta Chi, struggling with calculus, and finding intellectual transformation through Professor Colston Warne's economics course and through summer 1964 work in Harlem with Julian T. Houston and a young woman nicknamed Pigeon (Norman Cousins's daughter), who introduced him to Marxist class analysis.

chapters.title: SRE, Operation Crossroads Africa, and the March on Washington
chapters.summary: Williams led Students for Racial Equality at Amherst after defending the Movement in an editorial. Marian Wright Edelman's campus visit led to a scholarship through Operation Crossroads Africa to spend ten weeks building a hospital extension in Kigezi District, Uganda. Returning by ship, he landed in New York the morning of the August 1963 March on Washington and attended with his brother. SNCC singers and the Freedom Singer Cordell Reagon connected him to the Movement core.

chapters.title: The February 1965 Reform-or-Revolution Conference
chapters.summary: Williams organized a thousand-person Movement conference at Mount Holyoke for February 12, 1965 titled Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution. He cold-called Malcolm X for the keynote at a thousand-dollar honorarium and Malcolm agreed; Ossie Davis and Michael Harrington also accepted. Malcolm was stranded in France in a passport dispute; Harrington was blocked by a New York ice storm; Ossie Davis performed all three planned roles in a single keynote. Malcolm X was assassinated nine days later at the Audubon Ballroom.
related_events: Civil Rights Movement: Reform or Revolution conference Feb 12 1965

chapters.title: Drive South to Selma and SNCC's Montgomery Operation
chapters.summary: In March 1965 Williams and six others drove from Amherst to Alabama in his father's Buick station wagon (loaned after a Volkswagen breakdown), avoiding arrest in South Carolina through Fred Arano's quick-witted cover story about a Florida spring fling. Arriving in Montgomery, Stokely Carmichael diverted them from Selma to a parallel SNCC operation distrustful of SCLC, with Ivanhoe Donaldson directing logistics. Williams describes mounted-police charges against marchers and the Latino Movement organizer who stopped retaliation against an injured officer to preserve nonviolent discipline.

chapters.title: Seven Days in Kilby Prison
chapters.summary: Arrested on a sit-down in front of the Alabama state house with the Confederate and American flags overhead, Williams was held in Kilby Prison's seventy-five-man bullpen for about a week. Worth Long stood on a table and led an immediate redistribution of dusty blankets — the first time Williams saw organizing produce material results within minutes. When Long was moved to solitary, Williams and Detroit SNCC member Stu House held the cell together for the remaining days. After Williams's bail money arrived from Massachusetts supporters, he reluctantly left and witnessed the march's final mass rally at the City of St. Jude with Harry Belafonte, Nina Simone, and Ivanhoe Donaldson in a yellow rain slicker.
related_events: Selma to Montgomery March final rally

chapters.title: Newark Community Union Project Organizing
chapters.summary: Tom Hayden recruited Williams to the Newark Community Union Project in summer 1965, declaring the Southern Movement over after the Voting Rights Act. NCUP had moved from working with Clinton Hill Neighborhood Corporation homeowners to lower-hill renters around bad landlords, store credit fraud, and police brutality (which CORE handled). Williams worked the central-ward stoplight block-organizing assignment with organizer Karenna. Office staff included Terry Jefferson and the husband-and-wife organizing team Bessie and Thurmond Smith. Williams attended Yale Law fall 1965–summer 1967 while returning to Newark each summer.
related_events: SDS Economic Research and Action Project
Stokely Carmichael replacing John Lewis at SNCC

chapters.title: Newark Rebellion 1967 and Witness Documentation
chapters.summary: Returning from Philadelphia's Black Power Conference, Williams entered a sealed Newark and survived a four-officer shotgun stop only because law textbooks identified him and his companions as law students. He directed VISTA-funded law-student volunteers in interviewing witnesses to state-police shootings, including a young man shot forty-six times in a south-ward bar. Amiri Baraka photographed the corpse and circulated flyers. Williams was called before a grand jury where he was asked whether the flyers were inflammatory. He had not yet been targeted by HUAC; a 1968 hearing surfaced his correspondence with Tom Hayden, accused him of cohabitation with Phil Hutchings, and tied NCUP to the Rebellion.

chapters.title: Medical School Agreement and Gibson Administration
chapters.summary: Williams and the Newark Area Planning Association, working with Louise Epperson's Committee Against Negro and Puerto Rican Removal, fought the proposed 200-acre College of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey land seizure. Yale planners produced an alternative nineteen-acre footprint. The 1968 medical school agreement under DCA Commissioner Paul N. Ylvisaker and Higher Education Chancellor Ralph A. Dungan produced sixty acres of community-controlled housing land, a construction-trades training corporation, and a Model Cities veto structure. Williams became Kenneth A. Gibson's first campaign manager in 1970, then Model Cities director. After he raised an additional fifteen-million-dollar Project Rehab grant, Gibson fired him over an interpretive dispute on five-and-a-half-million in federal expenditures; Rep. Joseph G. Minish's front-page Star-Ledger story was answered by a Williams op-ed at editor Mort Pye's invitation. Raymond A. Brown headlined Williams's rally. A federal prosecutor, told by the auditor there was no case, never indicted.

chapters.title: National Bar Association Presidency and Zimbabwe
chapters.summary: Williams's law-practice base widened through O. T. Wells's invitation to plan the 1973 NBA convention. Working with the Young Lawyers Division and women lawyers, he became NBA's youngest president in 1978 at age thirty-four. The NBA International Affairs Committee, including Julius LeVonne Chambers, Elaine R. Jones, and Theodore M. Shaw, produced a legal paper on the inadequacy of the proposed Zimbabwe-Rhodesia constitution. SNCC contacts brought it before the UN African group and the Organization of African Unity; it was adopted by the UN General Assembly and translated into seven languages. Williams reports it was used to keep Jimmy Carter from lifting sanctions. Louis Farrakhan keynoted the 1979 NBA convention. Williams ran unsuccessfully for Newark mayor in 1982 against Gibson, lacking fundraising appetite.

chapters.title: Abbott v. Burke and Training the Next Generation
chapters.summary: After serving as Irvington town attorney under Mayor Sara Bost, Williams built an organizing institute at Rutgers-Newark to train parents around the Abbott v. Burke decision, which required New Jersey to fund urban schools at suburban levels under the state constitution's thorough-and-efficient clause. The institute also trains youth advocates in media skills, interviewing, and documentary production. Williams names electoral co-optation of street-organizing bases as the central failure to address — his manuscript Unfinished Agenda: Urban Politics in the Era of Black Power, then forthcoming from a university press, was his summary statement.
